articles.title: The greening of planes, trains and automobiles
articles.articleText:
Moving goods and people around the world is responsible for a large part of global CO2 emissions. As the world races to decarbonize everything, it faces particular problems with transportation, which accounts for about a quarter of our energy-related greenhouse gas emissions.

In 2018, road vehicles produced the largest share of transport emissions. Road passenger transport accounted for 45.1%, road goods for 29.4%, airplanes for 11.6%, shipping for 10.6%, rail for 1%, and other transport for 2.2%.

The fuels for transport need to be not just green, cheap and powerful, but also lightweight and safe enough to be carried around. Each mode of transport has its specific fuel needs, and much is still to be settled.

Planes may need synthetic hydrocarbons made from recycled air. Cars can use batteries and new solid-state batteries may go farther on a charge. Trains can often be electrified, trucks may use hydrogen fuel cells, and ships may use liquid ammonia.

This energy transition is global, and the amount of renewable energy the world will need is huge. Keith Wipke says the world needs to speed up the development of green energy, and renewables are likely to be used.

articles.title: In my ninth-grade writing class
articles.articleText:
In my ninth-grade writing class, I met a cowboy who saved his town, a strict father, and a modern-day Juliet. These people were characters created by my students, and I wondered how the students knew their subjects so well.

Things were different for their first essay, which asked why writing is important. Most essays were less than one page, and few contained a clear thesis statement. I was shocked.

Then I realized that the problem was the question itself. Students could have written pages about the necessity of computers, but writing itself did not strike them as important. This would have to change.

When a new unit started, I asked students to write a persuasive piece on a health-related topic. Later, a personal narrative and a creative-writing workshop followed. The students found these assignments much more interesting.

The results were staggering. Students wrote long stories with characters that broadened my view and touched my heart. By June, I understood writing's most powerful significance: it connects people and teaches us what it means to be human.

articles.title: One August afternoon
articles.articleText:
One August afternoon, I sat in my kitchen staring at a glass vase that had not seen daylight since my wedding.

My husband and I had sold our house and were busy emptying out the beloved home our family had spent 23 years filling up. We chose key items for the apartment, donated what we could, and rented a place to store important objects.

That left a house still stuffed with things that were not particularly valuable but did not belong in a landfill. The objects needed another chance instead of becoming waste.

I posted a picture of the vase online for ten dollars. After messages asking for more photos or a lower price, I changed the price to zero and wrote, “I hate this vase. Maybe you will not.” A woman soon left happily with it.

Encouraged, I posted more. My daily posts and the visits I received became a precious ray of light in the chaos of moving. Each exchange helped spare the landfill and pleased someone I might never have encountered.

articles.title: A Habitat Restoration Team
articles.articleText:
The Habitat Restoration Team helps restore and protect Marin's natural areas from the Marin Headlands to Bolinas Ridge. Volunteers explore beautiful park sites while removing invasive plants, planting in winter, and collecting seeds.

Habitat Restoration Team volunteers play a vital role in restoring sensitive resources and protecting endangered species across the ridges and valleys.

Groups of five or more require special arrangements and must be confirmed in advance. Participants should review the list of available projects and fill out the group project request form.

Volunteers aged 10 and over are welcome. Youth under 15 should read the policy guidelines, and volunteers under 18 must have the parent or guardian approval section signed.

The team works rain or shine. Volunteers should wear clothes that can get dirty and bring layers, a raincoat, water, sunscreen, and lunch. Training and tools are provided. Upcoming events meet at Battery Alexander Trailhead, Stinson Beach Parking Lot, and Coyote Ridge Trailhead.

articles.title: ARTS FIRST festival
articles.articleText:
Each ARTS FIRST festival is a unique annual celebration of the Harvard community's artistic creativity.

Visitors are invited to join the coming ARTS FIRST. The festival showcases creativity through performances, art exhibitions, and art-making activities. It is a public event for Harvard and community members of all ages.

In Light Awash in Watercolor, visitors learn about the materials and qualities of watercolor paint with experts from the Harvard Art Museums Materials Lab. They can try painting tricks used by artists in the exhibition.

In Spineless Artists: Invertebrate Creativity, visitors learn how insects and other invertebrates dance, inspire fashion, and create art. They also make their own spineless artists out of craft materials.

Wheel Throwing lets visitors watch potters demonstrate the potter's wheel and then create their own masterpiece. Knitting and Pom-Pom Making invites visitors to make pom-poms and tassels or learn to knit.

articles.title: Microplastics in tap water
articles.articleText:
Microplastics have become a common source of pollution across the Earth. They have settled in the deep sea and on the Himalayas, stuck inside volcanic rocks, filled the stomachs of seabirds, and even fallen in fresh Antarctic snow.

New research suggests that boiling and filtering tap water may significantly reduce microplastics. Researchers found that boiling tap water for five minutes and then filtering it after cooling could remove at least 80 percent of its microplastics.

The process relies on calcium carbonate in the water trapping the plastics. In hard water with 300 milligrams of calcium carbonate, boiling led to an almost 90 percent drop in plastics, but softer water showed a much smaller drop.

The research did not include all types of plastics. The team focused only on three common types and did not study other chemicals previously found in water, such as vinyl chloride.

The findings show a possible path for reducing microplastic exposure. Bottled water may contain much more microplastics than tap water, and researchers suggest looking into drinking water treatment plants so they remove microplastics.

articles.title: Go at the Jiusha Art Museum
articles.articleText:
An exhibition at the Jiusha Art Museum in Shanghai is featuring artwork inspired by Go, or weiqi in Chinese, which originated in China more than 4,000 years ago.

Go is one of the earliest binary-based games. The movements of the black and white pieces reflect the basic ideas of Eastern philosophy.

The exhibition brings together Go culture, cutting-edge technology, and contemporary art. It presents the abstract Go game and AI in a visual context to initiate dialogues with minimalist art, conceptual art, and expressionism.

In a Go game, each move should serve a long-term goal. A player may guide the opponent into a trap until they lose, and the players’ personalities are revealed during the game.

A decent winner tries to beat the opponent by only one or two points as a gesture of respect. The balance between black and white pieces and the strategic placement of the pieces inspired artists to create oil paintings, sculptures, digital pictures, and prints.

articles.title: Work, gender and physical activity
articles.articleText:
A detailed new study of U.S. physical activity patterns shows that men who work full-time, whether their jobs are active or sedentary, get more exercise than healthy working-age men without a job.

Researchers at the National Institutes of Health used survey data from about 1,800 working-age adults. Participants answered questions about lifestyle and work habits and wore an accelerometer to measure physical activity over several days.

The data provides a rare opportunity to measure how much activity Americans actually do. People in active jobs do more physical activity on weekdays than people in sedentary jobs, but the shift toward sedentary work still matters.

The surprising finding compares full-time workers with people who do not work. Men with full-time jobs did more activity than healthy non-workers, even when their jobs were sedentary. For women, the results looked different: women in sedentary jobs did less activity than healthy non-working women.

The study cannot provide much detail about the causes. Possible explanations include different abilities to pay for leisure activities, different attitudes about work and exercise, and the possibility that more non-working women are busy running the home after the children.

articles.title: An Unsung Hero
articles.articleText:
Need a break between classes or just a quick pick-me-up in the morning? The College Cafe has just that, and more.

Catherine Murphy, a cafe worker in a green shirt and black hat, makes sure that each customer gets exactly what they order. She goes back and forth between machines to make the drinks.

As customers grab their drinks, Catherine smiles and asks how their day is. Even when the line gets longer, she does not let it get in the way of her genuine conversations.

Murphy gets up at a quarter to five and drives thirty minutes every day to get to work on time. She likes making coffee for students because she knows they need it in the morning.

Murphy may not know that her smile is contagious and can make a student’s day better. Students say she always has a huge smile that puts them in a cheerful mood. Murphy enjoys working in the cafe and wants to continue doing what she loves.

articles.title: Movement: how to take back our streets
articles.articleText:
Although safety improvements may have been made to streets in recent years, transport studies also show declines in pedestrian mobility, especially among young children. Many parents say there is too much traffic for children to walk safely to school.

Dutch authors Thalia Verkade and Marco te Brommelstroet are bothered by these facts. In their book Movement, they call for a rethink of our streets and the role they play in our lives.

Life on city streets started to change decades ago. Whole neighborhoods were destroyed to make way for new road networks, and kids had to play elsewhere. Some communities fought back.

Canadian journalist Jane Jacobs moved to Manhattan and led a campaign to stop the destruction of her local park. Similar campaigns happened in Australia in the late 1960s and 1970s as well.

Despite these campaigns, many western cities were redesigned around the motor car. We invest a lot in roads that help us rush through, but we often fail to count the social costs when children cannot move safely around their communities.
